summary: Camille Fournier offers hard-won lessons for engineering managers transitioning to startups, drawing parallels to distributed systems challenges. She emphasizes that startup management lacks the invisible support systems of big companies—recruiting pipelines, established processes, HR infrastructure—forcing managers to build their own safety nets. The post covers coordination difficulties, trust-building, capacity estimation, feedback loops, organizational structure, and the inevitability of people quitting. Her core message is that the social challenges of management far exceed the technical ones, and survival requires building scalable processes while maintaining genuine connection with your team.
key_points: Big company managers don't realize how many invisible systems support them until they're gone; startup managers must build their own safety net of processes and standards
Coordination overhead is so massive that the industry invented microservices rather than force teams to work together effectively
As a leader, you must figure out how to get information about bottlenecks and problems without actually doing the work yourself—that IS the job
Engineers want to ship, and if they're not shipping, they'll either overengineer systems, quit, or both
People will inevitably quit regardless of how good a boss you are; your job is to identify systemic causes you can fix and keep going
money_quotes: You don't see the millions of invisible systems all around you that have set you up for success.

The amount of overhead that goes into managing coordination of people cannot be overstated. It's so great that the industry invented microservices because we'd rather invest engineering headcount and dollars into software orchestration than force disparate engineering teams to work together.
'The team is moving too slow' is the hardest problem you'll ever debug.
You must figure out how to get information about the bottlenecks and problems in the development process without actually having to do it all yourself. That is the job.
You may not personally be creating technical debt much anymore, but that doesn't mean you aren't creating organizational debt.
Engineers want to ship. This goes double for startup engineers. If they are not shipping much, they will start to complain of boredom, and go looking for ways to make trouble.
If you're bored, try doing it all 20% cheaper.
If you care about your team, helping them move on when they want to move on is a great honor.
key_insight: Startup engineering management requires building the invisible support systems that big companies provide while accepting that coordination, feedback loops, and people leaving are fundamentally hard problems you'll never fully solve.
